APRIL 16 2026 | REVOIR Magazine www.revoir.in 3

Imagining Tomorrow EDITORIAL Why wishes still Editor-in-Chief
MAGAZINE
Dr. Kamran Zuberi matter Social & Lifestyle Editor
Dr. Uzma Naaz
hat is it about a wish that feels so Art & Culture Editor necessary, even when we know it Dr. Monika Garg Wmay never come true? A child closes their eyes before blowing out can- Digital Strategy Editor dles. A patient whispers a silent hope before Vishakha Sharma surgery. Someone staring at a late-night ceiling makes a quiet deal with the uni- verse. These moments aren’t logical. They Social Media Editor aren’t always practical. Yet they persist ac- Sushil Kumar ross cultures, ages and circumstances. It’s tempting to dismiss wishing as naive, but Art & Graphics Director that misses something essential about being Vipin Agarwal human. At its core, wishing is less about out- comes and more about orientation. It points us toward possibility. Psychologists often EDITORIAL ADVISORY BOARD talk about hope as a cognitive process, a way of setting goals and imagining path- ways. But wishing is softer than that. It lives in the space before strategy. It’s the D. Bisht Shandilya (Former News emotional rehearsal of a better future, a way of trying on a life that doesn’t yet exist. Editor, United News of India, New And maybe that’s why it matters. In uncertain environments, the human brain doesn’t just seek facts. It seeks mean- Delhi), Prof. Amar Garg (Former ing. When we wish, we’re not ignoring reality. We’re negotiating with it. A student Vice Chancellor), Dr. Dharamveer from a small town who wishes for a different life isn’t deluded. They’re sketching a Mahajan (Former Principal), Prof. direction. Even if the path is unclear, the wish gives it shape. Without that first fragile Archana Sharma (Pol. Science), idea, action rarely follows. Prof. P.D. Sharma (Botanist), Shahid There’s also something quietly communal about wishing. Think of shared rituals, A Siddiqui IRS (Retd.) from New Year resolutions to collective prayers during crises. These acts create a sense of connection, a reminder that we’re not alone in wanting things to improve. Even in solitude, a wish carries echoes of others who have hoped before us, people SUBSCRIPTIONS who stood in similar uncertainty and still chose to imagine something better. Of course, wishes can mislead. When they replace effort, they become escape routes rather than beginnings. But most of the time, people understand this tension. Phone +91 20 40199102 They know wishing isn’t enough. Still, they do it. Not because they expect magic, but Contact us Survey Number 12/1, because it keeps a certain door open inside them, a door that reality alone sometimes Baner Road, tries to shut, especially during difficult, uncertain stretches of life. Pune-411045 (Maharashtra) In a world that often demands certainty, wishing allows room for doubt and imagi- Email mail@magazine.revoir.in nation to coexist. It says, things aren’t fixed yet. There’s still a chance, however Website www.revoir.in small, however distant. Perhaps that quiet insistence is what keeps people moving forward, even when evidence suggests otherwise. Legal Advisor I think we keep wishing not out of foolishness, but out of resilience. It’s a small Anil Bakhshi (Advocate) act, almost invisible. Yet without it, the idea of possibility itself might begin to fade, and with it, a quiet but vital part of what makes us human. And perhaps that’s enough. Not every wish needs fulfillment to matter. Some- times, the act itself sustains us, gently reminding us that even in uncertainty, we are Copyright © 2026 REVOIR (eZine), still capable of imagining, hoping, and quietly believing in something more. all rights reserved. This publication may not be reproduced in any form without permission.
Khzuberil Dr. Kamran Zuberi Editor in Chief zuberi@magazine.revoir.in
4 www.revoir.in REVOIR Magazine | APRIL 16 2026

IN-DEPTH
When Time Erases works lost to accidental fires. History is
filled with absences. And that’s where the urgency begins. Can technology Preservation is no longer just about maintaining what exists. It’s about cap- turing what might soon disappear.
rewrite the past? Enter the digital archivists In recent years, a quiet revolution has been unfolding. Teams of researchers, historians and technologists are using tools that sound almost futuristic. Laser What’s interesting is how this technology is reshaping scanners, photogrammetry, drones, im- mersive modeling. Together, they are our relationship with history itself. By Aarav Sharma building precise digital replicas of her- itage sites. 3D scanning, for instance, can cap- ture a structure down to the smallest hat happens to memory when The fragility of memory crack. Millions of data points create a the stone crumbles, the paint Walk through any ancient site and detailed model that can be explored, Wfades and the stories lose you can almost feel the weight of what’s measured, even restored virtually. It’s their keepers? It’s a question that lingers missing. A chipped sculpture. A wall not just a photograph. It’s a living quietly in the background of every that once carried color but now holds record. ruined monument, every weathered ma- only dust. Even the grandest heritage Consider the reconstruction of an- nuscript, every fading mural tucked into sites are, in a way, incomplete. They are cient ruins damaged by conflict. Using an old city lane. Time, as it turns out, is fragments of something that used to be archived images and 3D modeling, ex- both a creator and a relentless eraser. whole. perts have recreated lost structures with For centuries, we’ve accepted this slow Natural disasters, wars, pollution, astonishing accuracy. It’s not the origi- erosion as inevitable. But now, technol- neglect. The reasons for decay are many nal, of course. But it’s something more ogy is stepping in with an ambitious and they rarely ask for permission. than memory. It’s a form of continuity. promise: maybe loss isn’t final anymore. Think of cities that have vanished under There’s something quietly hopeful Or at least, not entirely. water, temples reduced to rubble, or art- about that.
APRIL 16 2026 | REVOIR Magazine www.revoir.in 5

“
A digital replica can
preserve the shape of
history, but the soul
still lingers in what
time has touched.”
When reality meets virtual space Virtual reality has taken this idea even further. It doesn’t just preserve. It reimagines experience. Put on a VR headset and you might find yourself walking through a temple that no longer exists, hearing ambient sounds that evoke its past, seeing tex- tures and colors restored to their original vibrancy. It’s immersive, almost unset- tling at times. You’re not just observing history. You’re inside it. Museums and cultural institutions are beginning to embrace this shift. Digital exhibits allow people across the world to access places they may never physi- cally visit. A student in one country can Probably not. Stories beyond structures explore a distant monument without But perhaps that’s the wrong ques- Heritage isn’t just about buildings or boarding a plane. A researcher can study tion. Maybe digital heritage isn’t meant objects. It’s also about intangible cul- intricate details without risking damage to replace. Maybe it’s meant to comple- ture. Languages, rituals, music, oral his- to the original artifact. ment. tories. These are even more fragile, Access, in this sense, becomes demo- often disappearing quietly without doc- cratic. Preservation versus experience umentation. But there’s a subtle tension here. Be- There’s a growing recognition that Technology is beginning to address cause while digital access expands preservation operates on two levels. One this too. Audio archives, digital story- reach, it also raises a question that’s is physical. The other is experiential. telling platforms, interactive maps. They hard to ignore. Physical preservation focuses on pro- capture voices, not just visuals. tecting the original object or site. It’s Imagine listening to a folk song Can a copy ever be enough? about conservation, restoration, careful recorded in its original setting, or ex- A digital replica can be perfect in maintenance. This work is slow, expen- ploring a digital archive of dialects that form. Every curve, every color, every di- sive and often constrained by environ- are no longer spoken. These are not mension captured with precision. Yet mental and political realities. grand monuments, but they carry just as something feels different. Digital preservation, on the other much cultural significance. Maybe it’s the absence of at- hand, is about capturing and sharing. It In some ways, digital preservation mosphere. The temperature of the air. ensures that even if the original is lost, gives these intangible elements a new The echo of footsteps. The unpredict- its form and story remain accessible. kind of permanence. ability of light shifting across a surface. The two approaches don’t compete. These are things technology still strug- They coexist. The ethical layer gles to replicate fully. In fact, digital tools are increasingly Of course, this shift isn’t without Standing before an ancient structure, supporting physical conservation. De- complications. you’re aware of its age in a way that’s tailed scans can help experts monitor Who owns a digital replica of a her- almost physical. You sense the passage structural changes over time, predict itage site? Who decides how it’s used, of time, not just intellectually but emo- damage and plan interventions more ef- displayed, or monetized? These ques- tionally. A digital version, no matter how fectively. It’s a partnership between tra- tions are becoming increasingly relevant accurate, doesn’t quite carry that same dition and innovation. as technology advances. weight. And sometimes, it’s the only viable There’s also the risk of oversimplifi- So, can it replace the real thing? option. cation. A digital model might capture
6 www.revoir.in REVOIR Magazine | APRIL 16 2026

“
Technology may
not replace the
past, but it refuses
to let it disappear
completely.”
And yet, the memory that stayed with me was not the digital version. It was the quiet stillness of the original, the sense of standing in a place shaped by time. The digital model helped me under- stand. The real place helped me feel. Perhaps that’s the balance we’re searching for.
Holding on, letting go So, can technology preserve what time destroys? In a way, yes. It can capture, doc- ument and recreate with remarkable ac- curacy. It can extend access, deepen un- derstanding and safeguard against total loss. But preservation is not just about sur- vival. It’s about meaning. And meaning often lives in the im- physical details, but it can’t always con- But it also raises a subtle concern. perfect, the incomplete, the physical vey cultural context or historical com- When history becomes editable, does it presence of something that has endured. plexity. Without careful interpretation, it lose some of its authority? Or does it be- Digital heritage doesn’t replace that. might even distort understanding. come more open to interpretation? It stands beside it. And then there’s the issue of access. There’s no easy answer. A companion, not a substitute. While digital tools promise inclusivity, And maybe that’s enough. ■ they also depend on infrastructure. Not Between loss and continuity everyone has equal access to high-speed At its core, digital heritage sits in a internet or advanced devices. So the dig- space between loss and continuity. It ac- ital divide quietly persists. knowledges that physical decay is inev- Preservation, in that sense, remains itable, but it refuses to let disappearance uneven. be absolute. There’s something profoundly human A changing relationship with the in that impulse. A desire to hold on, to past remember, to pass something forward. What’s interesting is how this tech- Technology, for all its precision, is nology is reshaping our relationship ultimately serving that emotional need. with history itself. And maybe that’s why it resonates. We’re no longer passive observers. We interact, explore, sometimes even A personal reflection alter digital representations. History be- I remember visiting an old structure comes something we can navigate rather once, its walls worn and its details fad- than simply read about. ing. There was a small plaque nearby This interactivity can deepen engage- explaining its history, but much of it felt ment, especially for younger audiences. incomplete. Later, I came across a digi- It makes heritage feel less distant, more tal reconstruction of the same site. It immediate. was vivid, detailed, almost alive.
APRIL 16 2026 | REVOIR Magazine www.revoir.in 7

COVER STORY
Why We Wish
Across cultures, languages and genera- The human instinctive. tions, the act repeats itself. It’s almost Psychologists often describe wishing as a form of mental simulation. We imagine a better outcome, a different possibility, a version of life that feels need to believe more aligned with who we want to be. But that explanation, while useful, feels incomplete. Because wishing isn’t just about imagining. It’s about feeling. Think about the last time you wished in possibility for something. Maybe it wasn’t grand. Maybe it was as simple as hoping a dif- ficult conversation would go well. There was probably a flicker of tension in your chest. A mix of doubt and belief. That It’s not always easy. Some days, wishing feels pointless. emotional blend is where wishing lives. And strangely, it’s not irrational. Other days, it feels essential. By Naina Kapoor Wishing as a survival tool It might sound surprising, but wish- child closes their eyes before amine it. Yet beneath its simplicity lies ing has roots in survival. Early humans blowing out birthday candles. A something deeply human, something didn’t just react to their environment. Acommuter stares out of a train tied not just to desire, but to survival it- They anticipated it. They imagined out- window, quietly hoping for a different self. comes, weighed possibilities and hoped life. A patient in a hospital bed imagines for favorable ones. That ability to men- tomorrow being kinder than today. The quiet habit we all share tally project into the future helped them Wishing shows up in small, almost in- Wishing isn’t something we’re taught prepare, adapt and endure. visible moments. It slips into ordinary formally. No one sits us down and ex- In modern terms, wishing is closely life so easily that we rarely stop to ex- plains how to wish. And still, we do it. tied to hope. And hope, research sug-
8 www.revoir.in REVOIR Magazine | APRIL 16 2026

“
Wishing is not escape; it is the
mind’s quiet way
of rehearsing hope.”
There’s also a vulnerability in wish- ing. To wish is to admit that something is missing. That there’s a gap between where we are and where we’d like to be. And that can feel uncomfortable. Maybe that’s why some people stop wishing altogether, or at least pretend to. They call it being realistic. Practical. Grounded. But beneath that, there’s often a quiet reluctance to hope and risk disappointment. Cultural rituals and collective belief Across the world, wishing isn’t just a private act. It’s embedded in rituals. People toss coins into fountains, tie threads on sacred trees, whisper hopes gests, plays a significant role in resil- Still, it’s worth asking ourselves: into the wind. These practices might ience. People who can imagine a better when does wishing help and when does differ in form, but they share a common future are more likely to work toward it. it hold us back? thread. They’re also better equipped to handle There are times when wishing be- They turn wishing into something setbacks. comes a substitute for change. When we communal. This doesn’t mean every wish leads stay in the comfort of imagined possibil- There’s a certain comfort in knowing to action. Of course not. Some wishes ities rather than facing uncomfortable that others are wishing too. That hope remain just that. But even then, they realities. It’s easier to wish for a better isn’t an isolated experience. It’s shared, serve a purpose. They create a psycho- job than to update a résumé. Easier to even if silently. logical buffer against despair. They re- hope for stronger relationships than to These rituals also give shape to mind us that the present moment, ho- have difficult conversations. something abstract. They make wishing wever difficult, isn’t the only version of But then again, even those wishes are tangible. You perform an action, ho- reality available to us. telling us something. They’re pointing wever small and attach your hope to it. It’s a quiet kind of resistance. toward what matters. It doesn’t guarantee anything, of course. But it creates a sense of participation in The line between wishing and The emotional weight of small your own future. doing wishes And sometimes, that’s enough. There’s often a subtle tension here. Not all wishes are dramatic. In fact, We’re told, sometimes quite firmly, that most aren’t. The psychology of possibility wishing isn’t enough. That action is A student wishes for clarity before an At its core, wishing is about possibil- what matters. And that’s true, to an ex- exam. A young professional hopes their ity. It’s about holding space for some- tent. Dreams without effort can become effort will finally be noticed. An artist thing that doesn’t yet exist. That might illusions. wonders if their work will ever reach sound simple, but psychologically, it’s But dismissing wishing entirely someone. These are quiet wishes, almost quite powerful. misses the point. private. They don’t demand attention, Humans are meaning-making crea- Wishing is often the first step. Before but they carry weight. tures. We’re constantly trying to make any plan is made, before any decision is What’s interesting is how often these sense of our experiences, to connect taken, there’s a moment of imagining. A small wishes shape our decisions. They past, present and future into a coherent brief, fragile thought that something else influence the risks we take, the chances story. Wishing plays a role in that narra- could exist. Without that, action has no we give ourselves, the patience we hold tive. It allows us to extend the story for- direction. onto. ward.
APRIL 16 2026 | REVOIR Magazine www.revoir.in 9

“
Every wish carries
a small, stubborn
belief that life can
still shift.”
There’s also a cognitive aspect here. When we wish for something, our atten- tion subtly shifts. We start noticing op- portunities, patterns, connections that align with that wish. Not because the world has changed, but because our per- ception has. The quiet persistence of hope values? Our fears? Our unmet needs? It’s a bit like tuning a radio. The sig- Even in difficult circumstances, There’s also room for balance. To nals were always there. We just weren’t people continue to wish. This persist- wish, but also to act. To hope, but also to listening. ence is, in many ways, remarkable. adapt. To imagine possibilities, while Think about individuals facing uncer- staying grounded in reality. When wishes don’t come true tainty, illness or loss. They often hold It’s not always easy. Some days, Of course, not every wish is fulfilled. onto small, specific hopes. Not grand wishing feels pointless. Other days, it In fact, many aren’t. transformations, but simple things. A feels essential. This is where wishing becomes com- good day. A moment of relief. A conver- plicated. Repeated disappointment can sation that brings comfort. A personal kind of ending lead to cynicism. People begin to see These wishes might seem modest, If you think about it, wishing is one wishing as pointless, even naive. They but they carry immense emotional sig- of the few things we carry from child- replace it with guarded expectations or, nificance. They provide structure to hood into adulthood without completely in some cases, complete detachment. time. They create something to look for- letting go. The form changes, the stakes But here’s the thing. The value of ward to, even if it’s just a few hours become higher, the innocence fades a lit- wishing doesn’t lie solely in outcomes. ahead. tle. But the instinct remains. Sometimes, a wish doesn’t come And in doing so, they make the pres- And maybe that’s something worth true, but it still changes you. It might ent more bearable. holding onto. push you to try something you otherwise Because to wish is to believe, even wouldn’t have. It might reveal what you Living with our wishes briefly, that things can be different. That care about more clearly than anything So what do we do with our wishes? life isn’t fixed. That there’s still room else could. It might even lead you some- Maybe the answer isn’t to control for change, however small. where unexpected, somewhere better them too tightly. Or to dismiss them as It doesn’t solve everything. It doesn’t than what you initially imagined. unrealistic. Perhaps it’s about learning to guarantee anything. But it keeps a door That doesn’t erase the disappoint- listen to them more carefully. open. ment. But it adds context to it. What are they telling us about our And sometimes, that’s all we need. ■
10 www.revoir.in REVOIR Magazine | APRIL 16 2026

INSIGHT
The power of voice
f you close your eyes and listen, who am I? It’s a strange question, but try How sound Ianswering it. Before you picture a face or imagine a personality, you’re al- ready forming impressions from sound alone. A voice reaches you before any- thing else does. It carries age, mood, confidence, hesitation, sometimes even shapes identity prejudice. And often, without realizing it, we respond to it faster than we do to facts. Think about the last time you heard A voice carries fragments. Memory, culture, emotion, someone speak on the phone for the first time. You probably guessed something intention. It shifts with context. By Pooja Mehta about them. Friendly or distant. Edu- cated or not. Local or foreign. Maybe you were right. Maybe you weren’t. But raphy, upbringing and belonging rolled Should I sound like myself, or should I the judgment came quickly, almost in- into sound. A person speaking English in sound like what’s expected? It’s not al- stinctively. Mumbai sounds different from someone ways a conscious choice. Over time, Voice is not just sound. It is identity in London, Nairobi, or Sydney. Even people adapt. Their voice shifts. Words in motion. within a single city, voices shift across change. Intonation smooths out. neighbourhoods and social circles. But somewhere in that adjustment, a The sound of who we are And yet, accents are often treated as question lingers. When we change how We like to believe identity is rooted something to be corrected, softened, or we sound, are we also reshaping who we in visible markers. Our name, our face, even hidden. That says a lot about how are? our clothes, our work. But voice quietly deeply voice is tied to identity and how does something more subtle. It tells a easily identity becomes something to ne- Between tone and emotion story without showing anything. gotiate. Beyond accent, there’s tone. The way An accent, for instance, is not merely For many people, especially in global we say something often matters more a way of pronouncing words. It’s geog- workplaces, there’s a quiet tension. than what we say.
APRIL 16 2026 | REVOIR Magazine www.revoir.in 11

“
A voice doesn’t just carry words, it carries history,
emotion and the
quiet weight of who we are.”
A simple “hello” can be warm, cold, distracted, or nervous. The words re- main the same, but the meaning doesn’t. That’s because voice carries emotional texture. It reveals what we’re feeling, sometimes even when we try to hide it. Psychologists often point out that hu- mans are wired to pick up vocal cues quickly. Long before written language became central to communication, voice was the primary medium. Survival de- pended on it. A raised voice could signal danger. A calm one could reassure. That instinct hasn’t disappeared. It’s still there, shaping how we respond to people. This is why leaders are often remem- bered for how they speak, not just what they say. A confident, steady voice can inspire trust. A hesitant one can create doubt, even if the message is sound. Teachers, broadcasters, customer service professionals, even podcasters, all rely on this subtle interplay between tone phone interviews or voice-based inter- Bias, hidden in plain sound and perception. actions. A recruiter might unconsciously We often talk about visual bias. But And then there’s emotion. It slips favour a candidate whose voice sounds auditory bias is just as powerful and per- into voice whether we invite it or not. confident or familiar. A customer might haps more subtle. Stress tightens it. Joy lifts it. Fatigue trust a service agent more if their tone A strong regional accent might lead dulls it. You can hear someone smile. feels calm and clear. someone to assume a lack of education. You can also hear when they’re holding The tricky part is that these judg- A non-native speaker might be per- something back. ments are rarely deliberate. They operate ceived as less competent, even when In a way, voice is one of the few below awareness. Which makes them their ideas are sharp and well-informed. things we carry that refuses to stay neu- harder to challenge. On the other hand, a “neutral” or tral. Consider how certain voices are la- globally familiar accent often receives belled. “Authoritative.” “Soft.” “Ag- unearned credibility. First impressions, formed in gressive.” “Pleasant.” These labels carry This is not about right or wrong. It’s seconds weight. They influence opportunities, re- about conditioning. There’s a curious phenomenon in lationships and perceptions of compe- From media to education systems, communication studies. People often tence. certain voices are repeatedly positioned form judgments about someone within For instance, studies have shown that as standard. Others are marked as differ- seconds of hearing their voice. Not mi- deeper voices are often associated with ent. Over time, these patterns shape our nutes. Seconds. authority, especially in men. Meanwhile, preferences and prejudices. Without visual cues, the brain fills in women’s voices are sometimes judged In workplaces, this can influence hir- the blanks. It estimates age, gender, so- more harshly, expected to balance ing decisions. In classrooms, it can af- cial status, even personality traits. It’s warmth with assertiveness, a narrow fect how students are perceived. In ev- not always accurate, but it feels convinc- space that’s not always easy to navigate. eryday conversations, it can determine ing. So, voice becomes more than expres- who gets listened to and who gets inter- This is particularly noticeable in sion. It becomes a site of expectation. rupted.
12 www.revoir.in REVOIR Magazine | APRIL 16 2026

“
Before we see a face, we’ve already decided something
about the voice
behind it.”
ourselves and think, “Do I really sound like that?” It’s unsettling, almost like meeting a version of yourself you didn’t know existed. But over time, that awareness can turn into something else. Acceptance. Even ownership. Voice is not fixed. It evolves with ex- perience. People become more confident speakers. They learn to articulate better. They adapt to different contexts. But ideally, without losing what makes their voice distinct. For some, this journey is deeply per- sonal. It might involve reclaiming an ac- cent they once tried to hide. Or learning to speak up after years of staying quiet. Or simply becoming comfortable with how they sound. There’s a quiet power in that. Not the kind that dominates a room, but the kind that feels grounded and real. Because ultimately, voice is not about perfection. It’s about presence.
And yet, awareness is growing. often strips away. You hear hesitation. A closing thought There’s a slow but steady shift toward You sense enthusiasm. You catch the If you go back to that opening ques- valuing diversity in voice. Podcasts, dig- rhythm of thought. tion, “If you close your eyes and listen, ital platforms and independent media At the same time, technology is also who am I?”, the answer is never just one have opened space for a wider range of reshaping voice itself. Voice assistants, thing. sounds. Voices that once felt marginal synthetic speech and AI-generated A voice carries fragments. Memory, are now being heard more widely. voices are becoming more common. culture, emotion, intention. It shifts with Still, the journey is uneven. Bias They’re designed to sound human, but context, but it rarely disappears. Even in doesn’t disappear easily. It just becomes something still feels slightly off. silence, we hold onto it. quieter, like a background noise we stop Maybe it’s the lack of imperfection. Maybe that’s why certain voices stay noticing. Real voices stumble, pause, breathe. with us long after the words are for- They carry irregularities that make them gotten. A teacher’s encouragement. A Voice in a digital world believable. parent’s reassurance. A friend’s laughter. Interestingly, as communication be- There’s also a deeper question here. They linger, not because of what was comes more digital, voice is finding new If voices can be replicated or generated, said, but how it sounded. relevance. what happens to authenticity? When a In a world that often prioritizes what Text dominates much of our daily in- voice is no longer tied to a person, does we see, it’s easy to overlook what we teraction. Messages, emails, comments. it still carry identity in the same way? hear. But voice, in its own quiet way, They’re efficient, but they lack tone. We don’t have clear answers yet. But keeps shaping how we connect, how we This often leads to misunderstandings. A the conversation has begun. judge and how we understand each sentence meant casually might come ac- other. ross as rude. A joke might fall flat. Finding and owning your voice And perhaps, if we listen a little more Voice notes, video calls and audio At some point, most of us become carefully, we might start to hear not just platforms have started to fill that gap. aware of our own voice in a new way. others, but ourselves, a bit more clearly They bring back the nuance that text Maybe it’s when we hear a recording of too. ■
APRIL 16 2026 | REVOIR Magazine www.revoir.in 13

TRIBUTE
Asha Bhosle’s timeless sound
The voice A voice that refused to stand still Asha Bhosle’s journey was never about staying relevant. It was about staying curious. That distinction matters. She began in an era dominated by that outlived classical influences and traditional play- back singing. Yet, she never allowed herself to be boxed in. From early ro- mantic duets to cabaret numbers that carried a certain boldness for their time, generations her voice adapted not by force, but by instinct. You can hear it in the playful lilt of “Piya Tu Ab To Aaja” and the soft melancholy of “Dil Cheez Kya Hai.” It’s easy to reduce a career like hers to numbers. Same voice, entirely different emotional worlds. There was something almost chame- Thousands of songs, multiple languages, decades of leon-like about her singing. Not imita- work. By Riya Verma tion, but transformation. She didn’t just sing songs, she inhabited them. hat does it mean for a voice sense of loss felt unusually personal, Crossing genres, crossing time to outlive time? For millions, even for those who had never seen her What makes her longevity remark- Wthe answer has long been perform live. Perhaps that’s because her able is not just the number of years she Asha Bhosle. Not just a singer, but a voice had been a quiet companion ac- sang, but the number of musical worlds presence that seemed to shift effort- ross generations. It played in homes, in she entered. lessly with every passing decade, every taxis, on old cassette decks, and now The 1960s and 70s saw her experi- changing taste, every new rhythm that through streaming playlists. It grew ment with jazz-inspired compositions arrived at India’s musical doorstep. with people. In some ways, it never and Western instrumentation. By the 80s On her death April 12, 2026, the aged at all. and 90s, she was equally at ease with
APRIL 16 2026 | REVOIR Magazine www.revoir.in 15

ghazals and more introspective listeners stumble upon her songs and melodies. Then came the unexpected wonder, almost surprised, “When was turn. Collaborations with younger com- this recorded?” ience behind that journey. posers in the 2000s introduced her to a That question itself is a testament to She faced personal and professional new generation that perhaps first heard her timelessness. challenges, navigated a fiercely compet- her not as a legend, but simply as a fresh itive industry, and still managed to voice. A bridge between generations evolve. There’s a certain grit in that It’s rare, almost unheard of, for an Ask someone in their seventies about story, one that perhaps shaped the emo- artist to remain relevant across such Asha Bhosle, and you’ll hear stories of tional depth in her singing. sweeping shifts. Most voices belong to a radio evenings and vinyl records. Ask You can hear lived experience in her time. Hers seemed to travel through someone in their twenties, and you’ll voice. Reinvention without losing Very few artists manage to become a A lingering echo identity shared cultural memory. She did. Immediate, alive, almost Even as music technology evolved, Parents passed it down without even re- present in the room. from orchestras to digital production, alizing it. Slightly husky, expressive, capable And maybe that’s the quiet magic. fade into archives or memory. It con- of teasing lightness or carrying deep Her music never demanded reverence. It tinues to speak, to surprise, to comfort. emotion. It was never about technical invited listening. In the end, Asha Bhosle didn’t just perfection alone. But past, but as something that still feels, from feeling dated. Even today, younger what often gets overlooked is the resil- quite remarkably, like now. ■
16 www.revoir.in REVOIR Magazine | APRIL 16 2026

PERSPECTIVE
The art of letting go
Healing inclined toward attachment. We assign meaning to experiences, people and out- comes. These meanings become part of our identity. So when something ends, whether it’s a relationship, a career path or even a long-held belief, it doesn’t feel through release like we’re just losing something exter- nal. It feels like we’re losing a part of ourselves. There’s also fear. Not just of loss, but of uncertainty. Holding on, even to What if the hardest thing to release isn’t the past, but the something painful, can feel safer than stepping into the unknown. It’s a strange version of ourselves still holding onto it? By Anaya Gupta comfort, but a real one. People stay in jobs that drain them, relationships that hurt them, or patterns that limit them, here’s a quiet kind of exhaustion rarely framed as strength. It feels like simply because they know what to ex- that doesn’t show up on medical surrender. Like failure. Like losing pect. Treports. It lingers in late-night something we fought hard to keep. And And then there’s the illusion of con- thoughts, in the way conversations re- yet, if you look closely at how healing trol. We convince ourselves that if we play long after they’re over, in the heav- actually unfolds, it almost always begins just try a little harder, think a little iness we carry for reasons we can’t al- there, not with resistance, but with ac- longer, replay things enough times, we ways explain. We call it stress, burnout, ceptance. might somehow change the outcome. maybe even heartbreak. But often, it’s We won’t. But the mind doesn’t easily something simpler and far more diffi- Why we hold on give up that hope. cult: the inability to let go. Letting go sounds easy in theory. In We’re taught to hold on. To relation- practice, it’s anything but. The quiet cost of resistance ships, ambitions, identities, expecta- Part of the reason lies in how our What we don’t often talk about is the tions. Letting go, on the other hand, is minds are wired. Psychologically, we’re cost of holding on.
APRIL 16 2026 | REVOIR Magazine www.revoir.in 17

Letting go in everyday life Letting go isn’t always about dra- matic endings. Often, it’s about subtle shifts. A young professional realizes that the career they once dreamed of no longer fulfills them. They’ve invested years into it. Walking away feels like wasting that time. But staying feels worse. Let- ting go, in this case, isn’t about quitting impulsively. It’s about acknowledging the mismatch and allowing space for Emotionally, it can manifest as anx- Acceptance is simply acknowledg- something new to emerge. iety, resentment or a lingering sense of ing reality as it is, not as we wish it to Or take relationships. Not all of them being stuck. Physically, it can show up be. It’s saying, “This happened,” or end abruptly. Some fade. Some change as fatigue, tension, even sleep distur- “This is no longer working,” without shape. Letting go might mean releasing bances. Over time, resistance becomes immediately trying to fix, deny or es- expectations, accepting that a person a kind of internal friction. It drains cape it. can’t meet certain needs, or simply al- energy without moving us forward. That’s where healing begins. lowing distance without bitterness. Think about someone who can’t let Philosophically, many traditions Even on a smaller scale, we face op- go of a past mistake. They replay it have pointed to this idea. Not as pas- portunities to let go every day. A harsh endlessly, dissecting every detail, imag- sive resignation, but as an active rec- comment. A delayed plan. A version of ining alternative outcomes. The event ognition of truth. When we stop argu- the day that didn’t unfold as expected. itself may have lasted minutes. The ing with reality, we free up mental and Holding on to these moments adds mental loop can last years. Or consider emotional energy that was previously weight. Releasing them, even partially, someone holding onto a relationship spent on resistance. creates lightness. that has already ended in every mean- Psychologists often describe this as It’s not about becoming indifferent. ingful way, except on paper. The at- a shift from avoidance to awareness. It’s about becoming less entangled. tachment keeps them from fully engag- Instead of pushing discomfort away, we ing with the present. allow ourselves to experience it. It’s The role of identity In both cases, the past continues to uncomfortable, yes. Sometimes deeply One of the most overlooked aspects occupy space that the present desper- so. But it’s also temporary. Emotions, of letting go is identity. ately needs. when acknowledged, tend to move. We often define ourselves by our When suppressed, they tend to linger. roles and experiences. “I’m the one who Acceptance isn’t giving up Acceptance, then, isn’t the end of always succeeds.” “I’m the one who There’s a common misunderstanding effort. It’s the beginning of a different stays.” “I’m the one who fixes things.” that acceptance means approval. It kind of effort, one that’s aligned with These narratives can be empowering, doesn’t. reality rather than in conflict with it. but they can also trap us.
18 www.revoir.in REVOIR Magazine | APRIL 16 2026

The paradox of release Here’s the strange thing about let- ting go: it often gives us back more than it takes away. When we release control, we often gain clarity. When we let go of rigid expectations, we become more open to possibility. When we stop clinging to the past, we become more present. It’s not immediate. And it’s rarely complete. Letting go tends to happen in layers. You might think you’ve moved on, only to find remnants of attachment resurfacing later. That’s normal. It doesn’t mean you’ve failed. It means you’re human. Healing isn’t linear. It circles back, pauses, moves forward again. 1. Notice what you’re holding onto A quieter kind of strength This sounds ob- We tend to admire visible vious, but it’s often strength. The kind that pushes overlooked. What through, that endures, that refuses to thoughts keep re- break. But there’s another kind of turning? What situ- strength, quieter and less celebrated. ations feel unre- The strength to release. solved? Awareness It shows up in small decisions. In is the first step. choosing not to respond to something that once would have triggered you. 2. Name the In accepting an apology you may emotion never receive. In allowing yourself to Is it anger, sad- outgrow something without guilt. ness, regret, fear? It doesn’t make headlines. But it Naming emotions changes lives, slowly, from the inside can reduce their in- out. tensity. It brings clarity to what might Closing reflection otherwise feel like a Letting go isn’t a single moment. vague heaviness. It’s a series of choices. Some con- scious, some almost instinctive. And 3. Allow the feeling it’s rarely perfect. without rushing to There are days when you’ll feel fix it lighter, as if something has finally This is where shifted. And there are days when the many people strug- weight returns, unexpectedly. That’s When a situation challenges that iden- gle. The instinct is to solve, distract or part of the process. tity, letting go can feel like losing our- suppress. Instead, try sitting with the feel- Maybe the goal isn’t to master selves. But in reality, it’s often an oppor- ing, even briefly. It won’t last forever, letting go completely. Maybe it’s to tunity to expand. even if it feels like it might. become a little more willing, a little Consider someone who’s always been more open to the idea that not ev- seen as strong, the one others rely on. Let- 4. Question the story erything needs to be held onto. ting go, for them, might mean admitting What are you telling yourself about Because sometimes, the healing vulnerability. Asking for help. Redefin- the situation? Is it entirely accurate? Are we’re searching for isn’t found in ing strength not as constant resilience, but there other perspectives? This doesn’t fixing what’s broken or reclaiming as the ability to adapt. mean invalidating your experience, but it what’s lost. It’s found in the simple, It’s uncomfortable. It disrupts the does mean loosening the grip of a single difficult act of release. story. But it also makes room for a more narrative. And in that release, something nuanced, flexible sense of self. quieter begins to grow. Not immedi- 5. Choose what to carry forward ately visible, not dramatic, but steady. How to begin releasing Letting go doesn’t mean forgetting. It A sense of space. There’s no single method for letting means deciding what’s worth keeping. A sense of ease. A sense, per- go. It’s less a technique and more a prac- Lessons, perhaps. Growth. But not the haps, of finally being able to breathe tice. Still, there are a few ways to begin. weight. again. ■
APRIL 16 2026 | REVOIR Magazine www.revoir.in 19

FEATURE STORY The disappearing night sky less. It doesn’t poison rivers or choke Light pollution sounds almost harm-
the air. It doesn’t leave visible scars on the land. But it spreads quietly, relent- When stars fade lessly, and often without resistance. Streetlights, billboards, glass towers, floodlit monuments, LED screens. Indi-
sirable. Together, they create a dome of from human life vidually, they seem necessary, even de- artificial brightness that washes out the faint glow of distant stars. There are different forms of this pol- lution. Skyglow, the most visible, is the diffuse brightness over cities that turns There are efforts underway. Communities advocating night into a dull orange or white haze. Glare from poorly designed lighting for responsible lighting. Scientists tracking changes in strains the eyes and reduces visibility. Light trespass spills into homes and hab- sky brightness. By Rohan Kapoor itats where it doesn’t belong.
hen was the last time you looked up and saw a sky full Wof stars, not just a handful struggling through a hazy glow? For many people today, that question lands somewhere between nostalgia and con- fusion. They’ve seen stars, of course. A few bright ones, maybe a planet or two. But the vast river of light our ancestors called home, the Milky Way, has quietly slipped out of everyday human experi- ence. Not vanished from the universe, but from our lives. This isn’t about astronomy alone. It’s about what happens when something an- cient and shared fades without us fully noticing. Farmers read seasons in the stars. Sailors navigated oceans by them. Sto- rytellers wove constellations into myths that explained life, death and everything in between. Even those who never stud- ied the heavens still lived under them, night after night, absorbing a quiet sense of scale. The sky was a shared language. It didn’t matter where you were born. You could step outside, look up and feel con- nected to something vast and enduring. Today, that experience is becoming rare. Studies suggest that a significant por- tion of the global population, especially in urban areas, can no longer see the Milky Way with the naked eye. In some regions, children grow up without ever witnessing a truly dark sky. Not once. It’s not that the stars have dimmed. It’s that we’ve brightened everything else.
20 www.revoir.in REVOIR Magazine | APRIL 16 2026

and protected reserves, the stars still shine in their full intensity. There are designated dark sky parks and regions where artificial lighting is carefully managed. In these places, the Milky Way stretches across the sky like a lumi- nous river, just as it did centuries ago. But access to such places isn’t equal. For many urban residents, especially in densely populated regions, reaching a truly dark sky requires travel, time and resources. This creates a subtle divide. The ex- perience of a natural night sky becomes something you have to seek out, rather than something you simply live with. And when something becomes occa- sional, it risks becoming optional. And then there’s the cumulative ef- fect. As cities expand and technologies become cheaper, illumination increases. More light feels like progress. Brighter streets feel safer. But the unintended consequence is a sky that slowly dis- appears. What’s striking is how normalized this has become. Many people don’t re- alize anything is missing. If you’ve never seen a sky dense with stars, you don’t know what you’re not seeing. What we lose beyond the stars The loss of the night sky isn’t just aesthetic. It’s cultural, psychological and even scientific. Without the stars, we lose a subtle Can cities and stars coexist? A fading cultural inheritance but powerful emotional anchor. That It’s tempting to frame this as an Across civilizations, the stars have quiet moment of looking up and realiz- either-or situation. Either we have mod- shaped identity. Indigenous communities ing how vast everything is. That feeling ern, well-lit cities, or we preserve the mapped stories across constellations, doesn’t come easily from a glowing sky- night sky. But that’s not entirely true. embedding knowledge about land, sea- line. The issue isn’t light itself. It’s how sons and survival. Ancient texts from we use it. Greece, India, China and the Arab world A barrier to curiosity and science Better-designed lighting can signifi- are filled with celestial references that Astronomy often begins with simple cantly reduce pollution without compro- guided philosophy and daily life. observation. A child notices a bright ob- mising safety or functionality. Shielded As the stars fade from view, so does ject, asks a question, follows curiosity. fixtures direct light downward instead of a part of that heritage. Traditions that But if the sky itself is obscured, that first scattering it into the sky. Warmer color once relied on direct observation risk spark becomes less likely. temperatures reduce the harsh blue light becoming abstract, disconnected from While professional astronomy has that contributes heavily to skyglow. lived experience. moved to remote observatories and ad- Timers and motion sensors ensure light It’s like inheriting a language you can vanced instruments, public engagement is used only when needed. read but never hear spoken. still matters. Amateur astronomers, stu- Some cities have begun to experi- dents and casual observers form the base ment with these approaches. Not out of A quieter sense of wonder of scientific curiosity. nostalgia, but practicality. Reducing un- There’s something about a dark sky When the sky disappears, so does an necessary lighting saves energy, lowers that recalibrates the mind. It makes ev- accessible entry point into science. costs and improves quality of life. eryday concerns feel smaller, not in a There’s also a growing awareness dismissive way, but in a grounding one. The uneven geography of that darkness has value. Not as absence, Urban life rarely offers that perspec- darkness but as a resource. Something to be pro- tive. We’re surrounded by constant stim- The irony is that the night sky hasn’t tected, like clean air or quiet spaces. ulation, noise, light, urgency. The night disappeared everywhere. It’s just be- Still, change is slow. Lighting is no longer signals rest or reflection. It’s come unevenly distributed. deeply embedded in how we design and just an extension of day. In remote deserts, high mountains experience urban environments. Shifting
APRIL 16 2026 | REVOIR Magazine www.revoir.in 21

that mindset requires not just policy, but public understanding. Relearning how to look up Perhaps the most overlooked aspect of this issue is behavioral. Even in places where a few stars are visible, many people rarely look up. Our attention is directed downward, to- ward screens, streets, immediate sur- roundings. The sky becomes peripheral. Reconnecting with the night sky doesn’t always require a remote expedi- tion. Sometimes it begins with small acts. Turning off unnecessary lights. Stepping outside for a few minutes. Let- ting your eyes adjust to the dark. At first, you might see only a hand- ful of stars. Then a few more. Grad- ually, patterns emerge. A sense of depth returns. It’s a quiet process. Easy to dismiss, but surprisingly meaningful.
A personal reflection on vanishing light There’s a particular kind of memory that surfaces when people talk about the night sky. A childhood trip to a village. A power outage that revealed an unex- pected sky. A moment during travel when the stars felt overwhelming in their number. These memories tend to carry a cer- tain softness. Not dramatic, not life- changing in an obvious way. Just quietly significant. What’s striking is how often they’re framed in the past tense. It’s not that such experiences are gone forever. But they’re becoming less common, less accessible, less woven into everyday life. And that raises a simple question. What happens when future generations don’t have those memories at all?
Holding on to what we can still see The disappearing night sky isn’t an irreversible loss. Not yet. There are efforts underway. Com- munities advocating for responsible lighting. Scientists tracking changes in sky brightness. Educators bringing as- tronomy into classrooms in new ways. Travelers seeking out dark sky destina- tions and sharing their experiences. Because the sky hasn’t really gone you’ll step outside, look up and catch a But beyond organized efforts, there’s anywhere. It’s still there, above the glimpse of what’s been fading. Not a also a quieter responsibility. To notice. haze, waiting. full sky, perhaps. Just a few more stars To care. To recognize that something as The real question is whether we’ll than usual. seemingly distant as the stars is still make space for it again. Sometimes, that’s enough to remind part of our shared environment. And maybe, on some clear night, us what we’ve been missing. ■
22 www.revoir.in REVOIR Magazine | APRIL 16 2026

DEEP DIVE
Blue Light Nation
three. Somewhere along the way, human time stopped being tied to the sun and Technology started orbiting around devices. And per- haps the most curious part is this: we don’t always feel in control of it.
The science behind the glow rewiring fact, it’s everywhere. Sunlight contains a Blue light isn’t inherently bad. In significant amount of it and during the day, it helps us stay alert, focused and awake. It tells our brain that it’s time to be active. human time light appears at night, it confuses the But here’s the catch. When that same body. Our internal clock, often called the circadian rhythm, relies heavily on light cues. As evening approaches, the brain When was the last time your day truly ended releases melatonin, a hormone that sig- nals sleep. Exposure to blue light from because the sun went down, not because your phones, laptops and televisions sup- presses that release. The body delays its battery did? By Aditya Joshi own shutdown process, even if the mind feels tired. So you lie in bed, exhausted but here was a time when evenings night and leisure stretches past what strangely alert, eyes adjusting to dark- had a natural rhythm. The light used to be considered “late.” ness that arrived too late. Tsoftened, conversations slowed We rarely notice when it happened. It’s not just about sleep duration. It’s and the body, almost instinctively, began There was no announcement, no turning about sleep quality. Studies have shown to prepare for rest. Today, that rhythm point. Just a gradual shift, one evening that late-night screen exposure reduces feels… negotiable. The glow of screens at a time. A quick scroll before bed deep sleep phases, the kind that truly re- has replaced twilight. Notifications turned into an hour. A late email became stores the body. The result is subtle but punctuate silence. Work seeps into mid- routine. A streaming episode rolled into persistent. You wake up feeling like
APRIL 16 2026 | REVOIR Magazine www.revoir.in 23

“
We haven’t just extended the day,
we’ve quietly
erased the night.”
the natural cycle of activity and rest be- comes fragmented. The erosion of natural rhythms Human beings evolved with predict- able cycles. Daylight signaled activity. Darkness signaled rest. Meals, move- ment and social interaction followed fairly stable patterns. Now, those cues are diluted. You can eat at midnight, work at dawn, watch movies at 3 am and attend meetings across continents before break- fast. Flexibility has its advantages, no doubt. It allows for creativity, accessibil- ity and new opportunities. But there’s a cost. When routines become irregular, the body struggles to maintain balance. Hor- mones fluctuate. Sleep becomes incon- sistent. Energy levels rise and fall unpre- dictably. Over time, this can contribute to stress, fatigue and even long-term health issues like metabolic disorders or you’ve slept, but not quite rested. Work, time zones and the always- mood disturbances. on culture It’s not dramatic. It’s gradual. A slow Dopamine loops and digital habits For many professionals, especially in drift away from what the body expects. If blue light disrupts the body, dopa- urban environments, the boundaries be- mine hooks the mind. tween work and rest have blurred almost Real lives, real consequences Every notification, like, message, or entirely. Global teams mean emails ar- Talk to anyone juggling a demanding update carries a small reward. It’s unpre- rive at odd hours. Remote work, while job and a digital life and you’ll hear fa- dictable, which makes it powerful. You flexible, often extends the workday miliar stories. don’t know when something interesting rather than compressing it. A young professional in Mumbai de- will appear, so you keep checking. Just A message at 10 pm no longer feels scribes checking emails at 2 am “just to one more scroll. Just one more refresh. unusual. In fact, it can feel expected. stay ahead,” only to wake up groggy and This cycle isn’t accidental. Platforms There’s a quiet pressure to respond, irritable. A college student admits to are designed to keep attention engaged. to stay visible, to remain available. Even scrolling through short videos until sun- Infinite scrolling, autoplay, algorithmic if no one explicitly demands it, the cul- rise, promising each night will be differ- feeds, they all encourage continuation. ture often implies it. And with devices ent. A parent notices their child strug- Stopping requires effort. Continuing always within reach, disconnecting re- gling to fall asleep, tablet still glowing feels effortless. quires conscious effort, sometimes even under the blanket. Over time, this creates a feedback guilt. These aren’t extreme cases. They’re loop. The brain begins to associate Students face a similar pattern. As- common. screens with stimulation and reward. signments, online classes, group chats, And often, they come with a quiet Quiet moments, once filled with reflec- digital resources, everything exists on awareness that something isn’t quite tion or rest, now feel incomplete without the same device that hosts entertain- right. People know they’re tired. They input. Boredom becomes something to ment. Switching off isn’t just about clos- know they should sleep earlier. But avoid rather than a space to inhabit. ing a book anymore. It’s about resisting knowing doesn’t always translate into And so, even when we intend to an entire ecosystem. doing. sleep, we linger. The body waits. The The result is a kind of temporal drift. Because the system around them mind resists. Days stretch longer, nights start later and keeps pulling them back in.
24 www.revoir.in REVOIR Magazine | APRIL 16 2026

Is technology the villain? ules. Others need strict routines. What
It’s tempting to frame this as a sim- matters is alignment, between what your “ ple story. Screens are bad. The past was body needs and what your habits allow. Our bodies still better. But that’s not entirely fair, or ac- And that alignment requires atten- curate. tion, something our current environment follow ancient Technology has brought undeniable doesn’t always encourage. benefits. It connects people across dis- rhythms, even as our tances, provides access to information, Closing reflections enables flexible work and offers enter- We live in a world that never really screens demand tainment that can genuinely enrich life. sleeps. Lights stay on. Screens stay ac- For many, it’s not optional. It’s essential. tive. Conversations continue long after modern ones.” The issue isn’t technology itself. It’s midnight. It’s exciting, in a way. Full of how seamlessly it has integrated into possibility. every hour of our day. There’s no clear boundary anymore. No obvious stopping point. The tools we use for productivity are the same ones we use for relaxation. The line between necessity and habit becomes blurred. And so, the challenge isn’t to reject technology. It’s to renegotiate our rela- tionship with it. Small shifts, meaningful changes Reclaiming time doesn’t require drastic measures. In fact, small, consis- tent changes often make the biggest dif- ference. Some people start by setting a “digi- tal sunset,” a time after which screens are avoided or minimized. Others switch devices to night mode, reducing blue light exposure in the evening. Simple habits like reading a physical book be- fore bed or dimming lights can signal the body that it’s time to wind down. Even something as basic as charging your phone outside the bedroom can create a buffer between intention and impulse. Workplaces, too, are beginning to recognize the importance of boundaries. Encouraging asynchronous communica- tion, respecting offline hours and pro- moting realistic expectations can help reshape the culture. For students and younger users, awareness is key. Understanding how these systems work can make it easier to But it also asks something of us.To resist them, or at least use them more choose, occasionally, to step back. To let consciously. the night be night again. To remember It’s not about perfection. It’s about what it feels like to be guided not by no- direction. tifications, but by a quieter, older rhythm. A different kind of awareness Maybe it starts with a simple deci- Perhaps the most important shift is sion. Turning off the screen a little ear- internal. To notice how you feel after a lier. Sitting in the dim light. night of late scrolling. To recognize the Letting the day end without extend- difference between rest and distraction. ing it artificially. To ask, gently, whether the way you’re It sounds small. Almost insignificant. spending your time is actually serving But sometimes, reclaiming time be- you. There’s no universal answer. Some gins with something as simple as allow- people thrive in unconventional sched- ing it to pass. ■
APRIL 16 2026 | REVOIR Magazine www.revoir.in 25

EXPLORATION
Not just a drink
Tea as a The chemistry in your cup
At the heart of tea’s effect are two key compounds: caffeine and L-thea- nine. On their own, they’re hardly unique. Caffeine is the world’s most chemical coffee, chocolate and energy drinks. L- widely consumed stimulant, found in theanine, an amino acid, is less famous but abundant in tea leaves, especially in green varieties. What makes tea different is how experience these two interact. Caffeine, as we know, blocks adeno- sine receptors in the brain. That’s the chemical responsible for making you feel sleepy. When it’s blocked, alertness rises. Heart rate may tick up slightly, What if the calm you feel after a cup of tea isn’t just thoughts sharpen and for a while, you comfort, but chemistry quietly at work? By Ishita Desai feel switched on. But caffeine alone can also bring jitters, that slightly anxious edge many coffee drinkers know too well. t usually begins without much that explains why tea doesn’t quite be- L-theanine works in a quieter way. It thought. A kettle hums, leaves unfurl have like coffee and why its effects feel crosses the blood-brain barrier and pro- Iin hot water and a familiar aroma gentler, more sustained, almost… con- motes the production of alpha brain fills the room. For many of us, tea is siderate. waves, which are associated with a re- routine, almost background noise to the Tea isn’t just hydration or habit. It’s a laxed but alert state. It also influences day. Yet beneath that simple act lies a subtle negotiation between molecules neurotransmitters like dopamine and se- surprisingly intricate chemical story, one and mind. rotonin, nudging the brain toward a
26 www.revoir.in REVOIR Magazine | APRIL 16 2026

Beyond stimulation It’s tempting to think of beverages only in terms of energy. How quickly they wake us up, how long they keep us going. But tea suggests a different framework. One where the goal isn’t just stimulation, but balance. In a world that often feels overstim- sense of calm focus. rushed. It’s not just cultural coincidence. ulated already, that balance matters. Separately, they do different things. There’s a biochemical logic behind it. The gentle interplay of caffeine and L- Together, they create something distinct. theanine offers a kind of middle A personal rhythm ground. Not too much, not too little. Focus without the rush Of course, chemistry doesn’t operate Just enough to stay present. This is where tea earns its reputation. in a vacuum. The way tea affects you You notice it in small ways. The combination of caffeine and L-thea- depends on context. Time of day, your Reading feels easier. Conversations nine doesn’t cancel each other out. In- sensitivity to caffeine, even how the tea flow without effort. Even silence be- stead, they seem to smooth each other’s is brewed can shift the experience. comes more comfortable. rough edges. The caffeine provides A strong black tea in the morning It’s not dramatic. It doesn’t an- alertness, while L-theanine tempers might feel brisk, almost energizing in a nounce itself. But it lingers. overstimulation, leading to a steadier, traditional sense. A lighter green tea in more balanced mental state. the afternoon can feel softer, more med- A final sip Some researchers describe this as itative. Matcha, with its higher concen- So the next time you make a cup of “calm alertness.” It’s not the sharp spike tration of both caffeine and L-theanine, tea, it might be worth pausing for a of energy you might get from an es- often produces a deeper, longer-lasting second. Not just to enjoy the taste, but presso shot, followed by an equally focus. to consider what’s happening beneath sharp drop. Tea’s effect is more gradual, And then there’s the ritual itself. The the surface. more sustained. You’re awake, but not pause it demands. The warmth of the Two molecules, working together in restless. Focused, but not tense. cup in your hands. These sensory details a way that feels almost intentional, Think of it less like a sprint and more matter, even if they’re not strictly chem- shaping your thoughts, your mood, like a long, even walk. ical. They shape how the body receives your pace. This might explain why tea has long and interprets the experience. It’s a quiet kind of chemistry. Easy been associated with reflective practices. Sometimes it’s hard to separate the to overlook. But once you notice it, Monks, scholars, writers, people who science from the setting. Maybe that’s it’s hard to see tea as just a drink need to think clearly without feeling the point. again. ■
APRIL 16 2026 | REVOIR Magazine www.revoir.in 27

ASTROLOGY HOROSCOPES April 16-30, 2026
